content_markdown: Tanya heats $100\text{ g}$ of a liquid with an electric heater which has a constant power output of $60\text{ W}$. After $100\text{ s}$ the rise in temperature is $40\text{ K}$. The specific heat capacity of the liquid in $\text{J\,kg}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$ is calculated from which of the following?
vibe_explanation: To find the specific heat capacity $c$, we use the formula $Q = mc\Delta T$, where $Q$ is the energy supplied. Since energy is power multiplied by time ($Q = P \times t$), we have $P \times t = mc\Delta T$. Rearranging for $c$ gives $c = \frac{P \times t}{m \times \Delta T}$. Substituting the values (converting $100\text{ g}$ to $0.1\text{ kg}$), we get $c = \frac{60 \times 100}{0.1 \times 40}$.
